title: Rug hooking on burlap
subtitle: Pulling wool strips through a hessian backing to build a traditional hooked rug
excerpt: Traditional rug hooking works by pulling folded strips of wool fabric up through a hessian (burlap) backing with a hook. The strips form loops on the surface; packed closely together, these loops build up the pile of the finished rug. This entry covers the equipment, the hooking action, and finishing the rug edge.
sourceNotes: Traditional rug hooking on hessian is documented across multiple public-domain household craft publications including The Modern Priscilla (1910s, public domain, Internet Archive) and British domestic craft manuals of the same period. The technique is a core element of North American and British folk textile tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: The common North American name for hessian, a coarsely woven jute fabric used as the backing for traditional hooked rugs. The open weave allows the hook to pass through the fabric easily.
The raised surface of a hooked rug, formed by the loops of wool strip pulled up through the backing. The height of the pile is set by the loop height; standard pile runs 8 to 12 mm.
A pointed metal hook in a wooden handle used to pull wool strips through the backing from below. The hook slides between the backing threads to grip and draw the strip upward.

content.text: Traditional rug hooking is one of the oldest textile crafts in British and North American homes. The tool is a
with a simple barbed tip; the material is wool cut into narrow strips; the backing is hessian (
is dense, durable, and completely washable when made from wool. For this project, a 40 cm square sampler is the practical starting size.

items.name: Wool fabric or yarn, cut into strips 6 to 8 mm wide

content.text: Step 1: Prepare the backing and design

Draw a simple design directly onto the hessian with a permanent marker. Geometric shapes, simple flowers, and bold borders are the most practical starting designs; fine details are hard to read at 6 mm strip width. Leave a 5 cm unworked border around the design for the finishing hem. Stretch the hessian tightly over the rug frame, securing it to the gripper strips at all four edges.

Cut the wool into strips 6 to 8 mm wide and 25 to 30 cm long. Wider strips produce a chunkier pile; narrower strips give finer detail. Mix colours within a design area by interleaving strips of different shades; adjacent colours blend optically in the finished pile.

Hold a wool strip under the hessian with the left hand. Push the rug hook through the hessian from above between two threads. Hook the strip and pull a loop up to the surface, about 10 mm high. Move two threads along the hessian in the direction of travel and push the hook through again, pulling another loop. Continue in rows, keeping all loops the same height. At the start and end of each strip, pull both ends up to the surface rather than leaving them looping under; trim these tail ends to match the pile height.

When the design area is fully hooked, cut the piece from the frame leaving the 5 cm border. Fold the border to the back of the rug and pin in place. Stitch the rug binding tape over the folded hem, covering the raw hessian edge on the back face. The binding tape prevents the edge from fraying and gives the rug a finished appearance when laid on the floor.

items.problem: The loops pull back through the backing when the hook moves to the next hole
items.cause: The hessian weave is too open, or the hook direction is pulling the previous loop rather than advancing
items.fix: Move the hook forward between threads in the direction of travel, not upward. Pulling upward at an angle is what draws the previous loop back. Work with a firm downward push between threads before lifting.

items.problem: The pile is uneven, with some loops much taller than others
items.cause: The strip was pulled to different heights on each hook action
items.fix: Use a small block of wood or a cut piece of card as a gauge: pull each loop up to a fixed height against the gauge before moving to the next hole. Consistency improves with practice, typically within the first 10 cm of work.
